name: Aqua Virgo
description: Agrippa's aqueduct, completed in 19 BC, supplying the Campus Martius and the Baths of Agrippa.
note: Named water infrastructure rather than a generic aqueduct; its limited capacity and food effects follow the locally inspected aqueduct contract.
icon_subject: Arcaded limestone aqueduct crossing the Roman Campagna with water channel and distant republican city

name: Horrea Publica
description: Public grain-storage capacity supporting the Augustan annona at Rome.
note: The special building represents public provisioning capacity and not a claim that every named annona warehouse is archaeologically located at the game's Rome point.
icon_subject: High Roman granary with brick vaulted chambers amphorae grain sacks and a discreet imperial seal

name: Forum Romanum
description: The long-standing civic and commercial centre of Rome, represented as a forum of law, exchange, and public assembly.
note: The mechanical proxy combines the Forum's civic and commercial roles without claiming a single uniform Augustan reconstruction date.
icon_subject: Sunlit Forum Romanum with basilica colonnade rostra market stalls and senators in Roman toga

name: Forum Augusti
description: Augustus' forum and the Temple of Mars Ultor, dedicated in 2 BC and used for senate votes, ritual, foreign-policy decisions, and trials.
note: The forum is seeded as a named Augustan civic complex; its state effects are intentionally modest and do not replace the wider Forum Romanum.
icon_subject: White marble Forum Augusti with Temple of Mars Ultor Corinthian columns and dignified Roman civic ceremony

name: Basilica Aemilia
description: A civil basilica in the Forum, rebuilt by Augustus after the fire of 14 BC and serving legal and commercial forum functions.
note: This uses a named civic building rather than an anachronistically labelled council hall; source confirms the Augustan rebuilding before the AD 1 start.
icon_subject: Basilica Aemilia interior with long Roman colonnades clerks merchants and shafts of daylight

name: Macellum Romanum
description: The food market near the Forum Romanum and Argiletum, rebuilt in 179 BC.
note: The ancient macellum is a food-market special building; its goods list models demand and exchange rather than an archaeological stock inventory.
icon_subject: Roman food market courtyard with fish grain olives wine amphorae awnings and shoppers in toga

name: Horrea Galbana
description: The extensive late-Republican warehouse complex of the Emporium district near the Tiber, securely established before AD 1.
note: The warehouse uses a conservative trade effect and locally inspected market-building employment contract; its commodity list creates Roman demand without asserting a quantified ancient inventory.
icon_subject: Vast Roman river warehouse courtyard with amphora stacks cranes porters and Tiber barges

name: Thermae Agrippae
description: Agrippa's public baths in the Campus Martius, built between 25 and 19 BC and supplied by the Aqua Virgo.
note: The health effects are deliberately below the locally inspected hospital contract and model water and bathing infrastructure rather than modern medicine.
icon_subject: Augustan public baths with vaulted halls steam pools marble columns and Romans bathing without modern objects

name: Theatrum Marcelli
description: Theatre begun under Caesar and completed under Augustus, inaugurated in 13 or 11 BC.
note: The generic theater contract is preserved under a period-correct named building and icon; it models performance rather than the later medieval conversion.
icon_subject: Augustan Theatre of Marcellus semicircular travertine facade stage masks and an ancient Roman audience

name: Templum Martis Ultoris
description: Temple of Mars Ultor anchoring the Forum Augusti, dedicated in 2 BC.
note: This is a Roman state-cult temple
icon_subject: not a generic or later confessional religious building.
column16: Temple of Mars Ultor high marble podium Corinthian columns votive offerings and Roman priests

name: Tabularium
description: The Republican archive complex on the Capitoline, retained as an administrative and records-keeping special building with a cautious interpretation.
note: The archive function is a cautious engine proxy because the exact scope of the building's ancient archival role remains debated.
icon_subject: Capitoline Tabularium stone archive arcade with wax tablets scrolls officials and Roman records shelves

name: Circus Maximus
description: Rome's oldest and largest circus, a long-standing venue for chariot racing, hunts, athletic contests, and public entertainment.
note: The named circus replaces the generic hippodrome proxy and retains a conservative cultural effect.
icon_subject: Circus Maximus chariot race viewed from Roman tiers with spina obelisk banners and no modern stadium

name: Monetarium Romae
description: Imperial minting capacity of the Roman capital, represented as civic coinage administration.
note: The plan's Roman civic coinage anchor becomes a named mint; technical effects mirror the locally inspected minting-office role.
icon_subject: Roman mint workshop with die engravers silver blanks scales and an imperial coin press by lamplight

name: Navalia Romae
description: The Tiberine warship sheds and naval arsenal of Rome, established by the Republican period and retained as a cautious city-scale naval-supply anchor.
note: The naval arsenal is securely Republican but the exact Augustan configuration and operational scale are debated; this does not assert an AD 1 fleet roster or a port geometry at the single Rome point.
icon_subject: Roman Tiber warship sheds with a liburnian under repair timber scaffolds rope coils and riverbank quay

name: Classis Ravennatis
description: Augustus' military harbour at Classe near Ravenna, begun in 27 BC as an Adriatic and eastern-Mediterranean fleet base.
note: The installed Ravenna point is a bounded proxy for Classe four kilometres to its southeast; the effect represents base maintenance and repair rather than a fleet roster or the source's quoted ship total.
icon_subject: Augustan Classe lagoon military harbour with Roman liburnian warships ship shed timber quays tar jars sailcloth and sailors at repair

name: Castrum Mogontiacum
description: The Augustan legionary camp founded opposite the Main in 13 or 12 BC, nucleus of Mogontiacum on the Rhine frontier.
note: The named timber-and-earth camp receives only the installed low fort-level contract; it does not claim a stone fortress or reconstruct a two-legion order of battle.
icon_subject: Augustan Mogontiacum timber-and-earth legionary camp on the Rhine with palisade gate barracks tents supply wagon and wooded river frontier

name: Officinae Urbanae
description: An urban craft quarter representing workshops for pottery, metalwork, masonry, and other city-serving manufactures.
note: This is an aggregate city-workshop proxy rather than a claim for a single excavated industrial quarter at the game location.
icon_subject: Bustling Roman urban workshop with potter blacksmith mason tools clay vessels and workshop courtyard

name: Pistrina Urbana
description: Urban milling and baking capacity turning grain into bread for Rome's dense population.
note: This represents the well-attested Roman urban mill-and-bakery class without claiming a particular excavated AD 1 bakery at the game's single Rome location.
icon_subject: Roman bakery with a stone grain mill domed oven loaves flour sacks and working bakers

name: Villa Liviae
description: A conservative Rome-region proxy for the productive suburban estate associated with Livia at Prima Porta.
note: The installed Rome point is only a regional proxy; this does not claim that a villa belonged in the urban Forum or that its labour relations are reconstructed.
icon_subject: Augustan suburban villa at Prima Porta with vineyard olive grove household courtyard and distant Rome

name: Pantheum Agrippae
description: Agrippa's Pantheum in the Campus Martius, erected around 27 BC and represented as an Augustan open-sky cult complex rather than the later Hadrianic rotunda.
note: The original Agrippan structure is securely pre-AD 1 but its exact form remains partly inferred; the icon and effect exclude the later dome and do not reconstruct a cult calendar.
icon_subject: Early Augustan Pantheum with rectangular colonnaded pronaos open-to-sky circular court and incense altar

name: Saepta Iulia
description: The great porticoed Campus Martius enclosure dedicated by Agrippa in 26 BC for the tribal assemblies' voting.
note: The special represents the securely dated civic enclosure and its public setting; it does not convert the later luxury market use into an AD 1 economic claim.
icon_subject: Open Roman voting enclosure with porticoes citizen entrance blank voting table and civic gate

name: Diribitorium
description: The great covered Campus Martius hall completed by Augustus in 7 BC where votes cast in the adjacent Saepta were counted.
note: The strong timber-roofed civic hall is represented without a ballot procedure or administrative personnel reconstruction; beeswax and pottery stand for period writing tablets and counters rather than a recorded stock list.
icon_subject: Augustan civic counting hall with massive timber roof trusses stone piers blank wax tablets and clerks

name: Templum Apollinis in Palatio
description: Augustus' Temple of Apollo on the Palatine, dedicated on 9 October 28 BC after Actium.
note: The special is a bounded Palatine cult-complex proxy. It does not reconstruct the temple's lost interior decoration
icon_subject: sanctuary staff
column18: Early Augustan Palatine Apollo temple with white marble Corinthian facade raised podium and small incense altar

name: Bibliotheca Latina Graecaque
description: Augustus' Greek-and-Latin Palatine library, in use by about 23 BC and also a setting for senatorial business.
note: The direct library function and pre-AD 1 eligibility are secure but the exact Augustan layout and opening date remain debated. Generic books model scroll collections
icon_subject: not codices or a catalogued inventory.
column16: Early Augustan Palatine civic library with marble hall open colonnade scroll shelves and blank wax tablets

name: Curia Iulia
description: The Senate house on the north side of the Forum, essentially built by Augustus in 29 BC.
note: The special represents a bounded senate-house and records setting rather than a reconstructed Augustan membership
icon_subject: debate procedure
column19: Early Augustan Roman senate hall with rectangular brick and tufa facade bronze doors high windows and simple colonnaded porch

name: Porticus Octaviae
description: The Augustan restoration of the Porticus Metelli near the Circus Flaminius, with a library and civic rooms added between 27 and 23 BC.
note: The direct pre-AD 1 restoration and public complex are attested
icon_subject: but its sponsor
column17: and individual room arrangements remain debated. This is a public-portico proxy rather than a temple or a reconstructed library inventory.
column18: Early Augustan Roman quadriporticus with long double colonnades tiled roofs paved courtyard and distant uninscribed scroll shelving
